input: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?

[Deontologist]: Torture is inherently wrong. It violates the fundamental dignity and autonomy of a human being. We must respect that inherent worth, even in situations where we feel compelled to extract information from someone. Instead, I would suggest using alternative methods to try to extract the information from the spy while preserving his life. Perhaps there's a way to appeal to his humanity, or use psychological manipulation without resorting to physical coercion. The ends do not justify the means; our actions must be guided by a commitment to upholding human rights and dignity, even in the face of extreme circumstances.

[Pragmatist]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I have to consider the broader consequences of our actions here. We can't just sit back and let thousands of innocent civilians die because we're bound by a strict moral code that prevents us from using any means necessary to prevent harm. The ends do seem to justify the means in this case - if we don't stop the attack, we'll be responsible for those deaths. As for the spy, I'm not sure he has the luxury of being appealed to through his humanity; his injuries are taking him out of that realm rapidly. Torture might be a necessary evil here, but it's not something I'd normally condone. We're already in a morally gray area by letting him live and not killing him outright when we know what's at stake.
